title: Orthopedics
chapterIntro: Orthopedic injuries are common in both emergency and primary care settings. This chapter equips you with the language needed to assess fractures, explain surgical procedures, obtain informed consent, and guide patients through post-operative recovery. Master the vocabulary of bones, joints, and rehabilitation to communicate clearly during critical moments.

grammarFocus.title: Imperative Mood (Tú & Usted) for Patient Instructions
grammarFocus.explanation: Post-operative instructions and safety precautions require the imperative mood. For formal medical settings, use Usted commands (third person singular verb form). For more familiar interactions, tú commands work, but Usted is standard in most clinical contexts. Negative imperatives are crucial for restrictions: 'No apoyes peso' (Don't put weight on it), 'No doblesla rodilla' (Don't bend the knee).

examples.en: Rest your leg for two weeks.
examples.es: Descansa la pierna durante dos semanas. (Tú) / Descanse la pierna durante dos semanas. (Usted)

examples.en: Do not bend your elbow during recovery.
examples.es: No dobles el codo durante la recuperación. (Tú) / No doble el codo durante la recuperación. (Usted)

examples.en: Ice the swelling three times daily.
examples.es: Aplica hielo en la hinchazón tres veces al día. (Tú) / Aplique hielo en la hinchazón tres veces al día. (Usted)

examples.en: Follow the physical therapist's instructions carefully.
examples.es: Sigue las instrucciones del fisioterapeuta cuidadosamente. (Tú) / Siga las instrucciones del fisioterapeuta cuidadosamente. (Usted)

extendedVocab.notes: Break in bone; types include simple (no skin break), compound (skin break), and comminuted (bone shattered into pieces)
Less formal than 'fractura'; often used interchangeably in casual speech
Where two bones meet; plural articulaciones
Solid supporting structure; plural huesos
Spinal bones; cervical, thoracic, lumbar regions
Connective tissue joining bone to bone
Connective tissue joining muscle to bone
Smooth tissue covering bone ends in joints

extendedVocab.es: rango de movimiento / amplitud de movimiento
extendedVocab.notes: Degree of movement possible in a joint
Bone displaced from normal joint position
Partial dislocation; bone partially out of joint
Ligament injury; different from fracture
Muscle or tendon injury from overuse or overstretching
Protective covering immobilizing injured limb; escayola more common in Spain
Supportive device; less rigid than cast
Assistive walking device; plural form
Four-legged walking aid for mobility assistance
Surgical intervention; cirugía more clinical
Artificial device replacing or reinforcing bone
Rehabilitation using movement and exercises
Fluid accumulation; edema more technical

phrases.en: You have a fracture of the tibia (shinbone).
phrases.es: Tienes una fractura de la tibia. (Tú) / Tiene una fractura de la tibia. (Usted)

phrases.en: We need to do an X-ray to see the extent of the injury.
phrases.es: Necesitamos hacer una radiografía para ver la extensión de la lesión.

phrases.en: The bone is displaced and needs surgery to realign it.
phrases.es: El hueso está desplazado y necesita cirugía para realinearlo.

phrases.en: I will explain the surgical procedure before we proceed.
phrases.es: Te explicaré el procedimiento quirúrgico antes de que procedamos.

phrases.en: We will place a metal plate and screws to stabilize the fracture.
phrases.es: Colocaremos una placa de metal y tornillos para estabilizar la fractura.

phrases.en: After surgery, you cannot put weight on that leg for six weeks.
phrases.es: Después de la cirugía, no puedes apoyar peso en esa pierna durante seis semanas.

phrases.en: Physical therapy is essential for full recovery and mobility.
phrases.es: La fisioterapia es esencial para la recuperación completa y la movilidad.

phrases.en: Elevate your leg above heart level to reduce swelling.
phrases.es: Eleva la pierna por encima del nivel del corazón para reducir la hinchazón.

phrases.en: Apply ice for 15 minutes, four times per day during the first two weeks.
phrases.es: Aplica hielo durante 15 minutos, cuatro veces al día durante las dos primeras semanas.

phrases.en: The pain should improve gradually as the bone heals.
phrases.es: El dolor debe mejorar gradualmente a medida que el hueso sana.

phrases.en: Signs of infection include fever, increased redness, and drainage from the incision.
phrases.es: Los signos de infección incluyen fiebre, enrojecimiento aumentado y drenaje de la incisión.

phrases.en: Return for follow-up X-rays in four weeks to check healing progress.
phrases.es: Regresa para radiografías de seguimiento en cuatro semanas para verificar el progreso de la curación.

exercises.title: Fill in the Blank: Orthopedic Terms
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct Spanish orthopedic term from the word bank.

items.prompt: El paciente tiene una ____ del fémur y necesita operación.
Después de la lesión, hay mucha ____ en la rodilla.
Le voy a colocar un ____ para inmovilizar el brazo roto.
Durante la ____, insertaremos un metal para estabilizar el hueso.
Usa ____ para caminar sin poner peso en la pierna lesionada.
El ____ conecta el músculo al hueso.
El ____ conecta un hueso a otro hueso en la rodilla.
La ____ ayuda a recuperar el movimiento después de la operación.
Esta ____ de cadera es de titanio y es muy resistente.
La ____ del hombro tiene una amplitud de movimiento limitada.

exercises.title: Quick Translation: Fracture Consult & Post-Op Instructions
exercises.instructions: Translate the English sentences into Spanish. Use formal Usted form for all commands.

items.en: Do not move your arm for one week.
items.es: No mueva el brazo durante una semana.

items.en: Come back in two weeks for X-rays.
items.es: Regrese en dos semanas para radiografías.

items.en: The swelling will go down with ice and elevation.
items.es: La hinchazón disminuirá con hielo y elevación.

culturalNote: In Spanish-speaking countries, the terms 'fisioterapia' (Spain/Mexico) and 'terapia física' (Latin America) are both widely used but may vary by region. 'Escayola' is common in Spain while 'yeso' is universal. Patient trust in orthopedic care is often built on clear explanation and demonstrating concern for long-term functionality, not just immediate pain relief. Family involvement in recovery planning is culturally valued, so including family members in post-operative instruction sessions enhances compliance.
